Work on the garden began in 1650, and took ten years to complete. The garden utilizes the gentle slope of the landscape of Kannon-in. A depiction of the garden is found the {{Nihongo|''Kannon-in ezu''|観音院絵図}}, or illustration of Kannon-in, published in the {{Nihongo|無駄安留記|Mudaaruki}} in 1858. The ''Mudaaruki'' is probably based on earlier works.[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/ ]{{in lang|ja}} The {{Nihongo|bekkan|別館}} annex structure in the garden is in a slightly different location in the ''Mudaaruki'' than what is seen in the garden today, as the Kannon-in garden was probably restructured during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. ==Branch temples== Kannon-in has two branch temples. *{{Nihongo|Seikyō-ji|清鏡寺}}, Tachikawachō, Tottori City *{{Nihongo|Enjō-ji|円城}}, Kokufuchō, Tottori City == Transportation == *8 minutes by taxi from {{ill|Tottori Station|en|Tottori Station|WD=Q858295|lt=Tottori Station|1=Tottori Station}}, {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West|1=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Sanin Main Line|Sanin Main Line]] *4 minute walk from the {{Nihongo|Yamanote Kaikan-mae Stop|山の手会館前}}, {{Nihongo|[[Kururi Bus Line]]|くる梨}} {{Nihongo|Red Course|赤コース}} from Tottori Station == See also == * For an explanation of terms used see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == Order in Buddhist pilgrimage == Kannon-in is the 32nd of the {{ill|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q987974|lt=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}}, a ''[[junrei]]'' pilgrimage route established in 1981 of 33 Buddhist temples in the dedicated to the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} Kannon. The route stretches across the [[:en:Chūgoku Region|Chūgoku Region]] of western Japan from {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane|1=Shimane Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}} prefectures.[http://www.kannon.org/reijyo/01/index.htm 名刹古刹37ヶ寺中国観音霊場とは]{{in lang|ja}} {{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mani-ji]]
(special)}} {{s-ttl|title=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage |years='''Kannon-in'''
#32}} {{s-aft|after=[[Daiun-in]]
#33}} {{s-end}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|WD=Q6594211|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|1=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)}} ==References== {{reflist|33em}} ==External links== *[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] [[en:Kannon-in]] [[arz:كانون ان]] {{Commons category|Jigen-ji Kannon-in}} [[fr:Kannon-in]] [[ja:観音院]] [[ko:간논인]] {{wikidata link|Q3192708}} [[Category:1632 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:17th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Gardens in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Tendai temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Kannon-in]][[en:Kannon-in]][[fr:Kannon-in]][[ja:観音院]][[ko:간논인]] [[arz:كانون ان]][[co:commons:Category:Jigen-ji Kannon-in]][[en:Kannon-in]][[fr:Kannon-in]][[ja:観音院]][[ko:간논인]] [[arz:كانون ان]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Jigen-ji Kannon-in]][[en:Kannon-in]][[fr:Kannon-in]][[ja:観音院]][[ko:간논인]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kannonin Tottori11n4592.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964|1=Tendai}}|prefecture={{ill|Tottori|WD=Q200731|1=Tottori (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.49608889|N|134.24155|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q193849|1=Avalokiteśvara}}|part_of={{ill|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q987974|1=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Tottori, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kannon-in | native_name = 観音院 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kannonin Tottori22s4110.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Garden of Kannon-in | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Kannon-in | location = 162 Uemachi, {{ill|Tottori (city)|en|Tottori (city)|WD=Q200731|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori (city)}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|29|45.92|N|134|14|29.58|E|display=inline|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tendai}} | deity = {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q193849|lt=Shōkannon|1=Avalokiteśvara}} | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm}} | founded_by = | year_completed = 1632 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Kannonin Tottori06s4s4440.jpg|thumb|Sho-in study hall]] [[File:Japan Tottori KannonIn DSC01905.jpg|thumb|Garden of Kannon-in]] [[File:Kannonin Tottori01o4592.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hondō|Hondō]] main hall of Kannon-in]] [[File:Kannonin Tottori04n4592.jpg|thumb|[[Sanmon]] gates of Kannon-in]] {{nihongo|'''Kannon-in'''|観音院|Kannon-in}}, formally known as {{nihongo|Fudarakusan Jigen-ji Kannon-in|補陀落山慈眼寺観音院||}}, is a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in Uemachi district of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]], {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Kannon-in was built early in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603 – 1868) and is noted for its Edo-style {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|WD=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}}.[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} ==History== ===Founding=== Kannon-in built in the early Edo period and its history is closely related to that of the {{ill|Ikeda clan|en|Ikeda clan|WD=Q2975277|lt=Ikeda clan|1=Ikeda clan}}. {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Tadakatsu]]|池田忠雄|}} (1602 – 1632), ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|WD=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain}} in {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} and lord of {{ill|Okayama Castle|en|Okayama Castle|WD=Q1013448|lt=Okayama Castle|1=Okayama Castle}}, died at a young age and was succeeded by his 3 year old son {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Mitsunaka]]|池田光仲}} (1630 – 1693). The [[Tokugawa shogunate]] named the infant Mitsunaka ''daimyō'' of {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}} in {{ill|Hōki Province|en|Hōki Province|WD=Q1053703|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} provinces.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Mitsunaka |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} In 1632, soon after Mitsunaka's accession to the position of daimyō, {{Nihongo|Senden|専伝}}, the fourth chief priest of [[Kōchin-ji]] in the present-day Okayama, sent his disciple Gōben to build a temple for the Ikeda clan. Senden ordered Gōben to build the temple in the scenic Kuritani area of present-day Tottori City,[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/7-kannonin.html 補陀絡山慈眼寺観音院]{{in lang|ja}} and charged him with building prayer temple for the veneration of the {{Nihongo|Shōkanzeon Bosatsu|聖観世音菩薩}}, or [[Kannon|Kannon]] {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}. The temple was named Kannon-in. A statue of the Kannon Bodhisattva, reputably carved from rock from the mountain of {{ill|Tottori Castle|en|Tottori Castle|WD=Q1073699|lt=Tottori Castle|1=Tottori Castle}} by the {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} (668 – 749), a Buddhist priest of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, was bestowed on the temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 ) |title=Kannnon-in |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-29 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} ===Move to Uemachi site=== In 1639 the temple was moved to its present location in the Uemachi of present-day Tottori City to serve as a temple for the use of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}. Mitsunaka was deeply devoted to the Kannon Bodhisattva, and became a patron of the temple. At this time the extensive {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|伽藍}} temple complex Kannon-in was planned and built, including its well-known garden. At this time Kannon-in was formally renamed Fudarakusan Jigen-ji Kannon-in. Mitsunaka's oldest son, the second lord of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|WD=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}, named Kannon-in a {{Nihongo|''kiganji''|祈願寺}} prayer temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Tsunakiyo |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The temple attained the high status of one of the {{Nihongo|eight prayer temples|八ヶ寺|hachigatera}} of the domain, a status it would retain throughout the Edo period. ===Later history=== After the abolition of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=han|1=Han system}} system Kannon-in ceased to be a temple under the patronage of the Ikeda clan, and the temple lost its rice stipend and any form of monetary support. Soon after local adherents of Kannon-in took over the financial support of the temple, support that continues to the present. The Kannon-in garden was designated as a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Place of Scenic Beauty|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} by the Japanese government in 1937.[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] == Garden of Kannon-in == The garden of Kannon-in was built is an example of an Edo period Japanese garden in the {{Nihongo|''Chisenkanshō-shiki teien''|池泉観賞式庭園|}} style, which literally means a garden of the "pond appreciation style". A ''Chisenkanshō-shiki'' garden is meant to be viewed from a fixed perspective from a single location, rather than a garden to stroll through and view from several angles.[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chisenkanshoushikiteien.htm Chisen Kanshoushiki Teien] In the case of the Kannon-in the garden is viewed from the {{ill|Veranda|en|Veranda|WD=Q693922|lt=Veranda|1=Veranda}} of the {{Nihongo|''shoin''|書院|}}, a hall used for the study of Buddhist {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}}s. Work on the garden began in 1650, and took ten years to complete. The garden utilizes the gentle slope of the landscape of Kannon-in. A depiction of the garden is found the {{Nihongo|''Kannon-in ezu''|観音院絵図}}, or illustration of Kannon-in, published in the {{Nihongo|無駄安留記|Mudaaruki}} in 1858. The ''Mudaaruki'' is probably based on earlier works.[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/ ]{{in lang|ja}} The {{Nihongo|bekkan|別館}} annex structure in the garden is in a slightly different location in the ''Mudaaruki'' than what is seen in the garden today, as the Kannon-in garden was probably restructured during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. ==Branch temples== Kannon-in has two branch temples. *{{Nihongo|Seikyō-ji|清鏡寺}}, Tachikawachō, Tottori City *{{Nihongo|Enjō-ji|円城}}, Kokufuchō, Tottori City == Transportation == *8 minutes by taxi from {{ill|Tottori Station|en|Tottori Station|WD=Q858295|lt=Tottori Station|1=Tottori Station}}, {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West|1=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Sanin Main Line|Sanin Main Line]] *4 minute walk from the {{Nihongo|Yamanote Kaikan-mae Stop|山の手会館前}}, {{Nihongo|[[Kururi Bus Line]]|くる梨}} {{Nihongo|Red Course|赤コース}} from Tottori Station == See also == * For an explanation of terms used see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == Order in Buddhist pilgrimage == Kannon-in is the 32nd of the {{ill|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q987974|lt=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}}, a ''[[junrei]]'' pilgrimage route established in 1981 of 33 Buddhist temples in the dedicated to the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} Kannon. The route stretches across the [[:en:Chūgoku Region|Chūgoku Region]] of western Japan from {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane|1=Shimane Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}} prefectures.[http://www.kannon.org/reijyo/01/index.htm 名刹古刹37ヶ寺中国観音霊場とは]{{in lang|ja}} {{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mani-ji]]
(special)}} {{s-ttl|title=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage |years='''Kannon-in'''
#32}} {{s-aft|after=[[Daiun-in]]
#33}} {{s-end}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|WD=Q6594211|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|1=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)}} ==References== {{reflist|33em}} ==External links== *[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] [[en:Kannon-in]] [[arz:كانون ان]] {{Commons category|Jigen-ji Kannon-in}} [[fr:Kannon-in]] [[ja:観音院]] [[ko:간논인]] {{wikidata link|Q3192708}} [[Category:1632 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:17th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Gardens in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Tendai temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Kannon-in was built early in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603 – 1868) and is noted for its Edo-style {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|qid=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}}.[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} ==History== ===Founding=== Kannon-in built in the early Edo period and its history is closely related to that of the {{ill|Ikeda clan|en|Ikeda clan|qid=Q2975277|lt=Ikeda clan|1=Ikeda clan}}. {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Tadakatsu]]|池田忠雄|}} (1602 – 1632), ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|qid=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain}} in {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|qid=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} and lord of {{ill|Okayama Castle|en|Okayama Castle|qid=Q1013448|lt=Okayama Castle|1=Okayama Castle}}, died at a young age and was succeeded by his 3 year old son {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Mitsunaka]]|池田光仲}} (1630 – 1693). The [[Tokugawa shogunate]] named the infant Mitsunaka ''daimyō'' of {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}} in {{ill|Hōki Province|en|Hōki Province|qid=Q1053703|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|qid=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} provinces.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Mitsunaka |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} In 1632, soon after Mitsunaka's accession to the position of daimyō, {{Nihongo|Senden|専伝}}, the fourth chief priest of [[Kōchin-ji]] in the present-day Okayama, sent his disciple Gōben to build a temple for the Ikeda clan. Senden ordered Gōben to build the temple in the scenic Kuritani area of present-day Tottori City,[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/7-kannonin.html 補陀絡山慈眼寺観音院]{{in lang|ja}} and charged him with building prayer temple for the veneration of the {{Nihongo|Shōkanzeon Bosatsu|聖観世音菩薩}}, or [[Kannon|Kannon]] {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}. The temple was named Kannon-in. A statue of the Kannon Bodhisattva, reputably carved from rock from the mountain of {{ill|Tottori Castle|en|Tottori Castle|qid=Q1073699|lt=Tottori Castle|1=Tottori Castle}} by the {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} (668 – 749), a Buddhist priest of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, was bestowed on the temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 ) |title=Kannnon-in |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-29 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} ===Move to Uemachi site=== In 1639 the temple was moved to its present location in the Uemachi of present-day Tottori City to serve as a temple for the use of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}. Mitsunaka was deeply devoted to the Kannon Bodhisattva, and became a patron of the temple. At this time the extensive {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|伽藍}} temple complex Kannon-in was planned and built, including its well-known garden. At this time Kannon-in was formally renamed Fudarakusan Jigen-ji Kannon-in. Mitsunaka's oldest son, the second lord of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}, named Kannon-in a {{Nihongo|''kiganji''|祈願寺}} prayer temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Tsunakiyo |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The temple attained the high status of one of the {{Nihongo|eight prayer temples|八ヶ寺|hachigatera}} of the domain, a status it would retain throughout the Edo period. ===Later history=== After the abolition of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|qid=Q841985|lt=han|1=Han system}} system Kannon-in ceased to be a temple under the patronage of the Ikeda clan, and the temple lost its rice stipend and any form of monetary support. Soon after local adherents of Kannon-in took over the financial support of the temple, support that continues to the present. The Kannon-in garden was designated as a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|qid=Q20982902|lt=Place of Scenic Beauty|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} by the Japanese government in 1937.[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] == Garden of Kannon-in == The garden of Kannon-in was built is an example of an Edo period Japanese garden in the {{Nihongo|''Chisenkanshō-shiki teien''|池泉観賞式庭園|}} style, which literally means a garden of the "pond appreciation style". A ''Chisenkanshō-shiki'' garden is meant to be viewed from a fixed perspective from a single location, rather than a garden to stroll through and view from several angles.[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chisenkanshoushikiteien.htm Chisen Kanshoushiki Teien] In the case of the Kannon-in the garden is viewed from the {{ill|Veranda|en|Veranda|qid=Q693922|lt=Veranda|1=Veranda}} of the {{Nihongo|''shoin''|書院|}}, a hall used for the study of Buddhist {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}}s. Work on the garden began in 1650, and took ten years to complete. The garden utilizes the gentle slope of the landscape of Kannon-in. A depiction of the garden is found the {{Nihongo|''Kannon-in ezu''|観音院絵図}}, or illustration of Kannon-in, published in the {{Nihongo|無駄安留記|Mudaaruki}} in 1858. The ''Mudaaruki'' is probably based on earlier works.[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/ ]{{in lang|ja}} The {{Nihongo|bekkan|別館}} annex structure in the garden is in a slightly different location in the ''Mudaaruki'' than what is seen in the garden today, as the Kannon-in garden was probably restructured during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. ==Branch temples== Kannon-in has two branch temples. *{{Nihongo|Seikyō-ji|清鏡寺}}, Tachikawachō, Tottori City *{{Nihongo|Enjō-ji|円城}}, Kokufuchō, Tottori City == Transportation == *8 minutes by taxi from {{ill|Tottori Station|en|Tottori Station|qid=Q858295|lt=Tottori Station|1=Tottori Station}}, {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=JR West|1=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Sanin Main Line|Sanin Main Line]] *4 minute walk from the {{Nihongo|Yamanote Kaikan-mae Stop|山の手会館前}}, {{Nihongo|[[Kururi Bus Line]]|くる梨}} {{Nihongo|Red Course|赤コース}} from Tottori Station == See also == * For an explanation of terms used see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == Order in Buddhist pilgrimage == Kannon-in is the 32nd of the {{ill|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q987974|lt=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}}, a ''[[junrei]]'' pilgrimage route established in 1981 of 33 Buddhist temples in the dedicated to the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} Kannon. The route stretches across the [[:en:Chūgoku Region|Chūgoku Region]] of western Japan from {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane|1=Shimane Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}} prefectures.[http://www.kannon.org/reijyo/01/index.htm 名刹古刹37ヶ寺中国観音霊場とは]{{in lang|ja}} {{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mani-ji]]
(special)}} {{s-ttl|title=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage |years='''Kannon-in'''
#32}} {{s-aft|after=[[Daiun-in]]
#33}} {{s-end}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|qid=Q6594211|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|1=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)}} ==References== {{reflist|33em}} ==External links== *[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] [[en:Kannon-in]] [[arz:كانون ان]] {{Commons category|Jigen-ji Kannon-in}} [[fr:Kannon-in]] [[ja:観音院]] [[ko:간논인]] {{wikidata link|Q3192708}} [[Category:1632 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:17th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Gardens in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Tendai temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

hall of Kannon-in]] [[File:Kannonin Tottori04n4592.jpg|thumb|[[Sanmon]] gates of Kannon-in]] {{nihongo|'''Kannon-in'''|観音院|Kannon-in}}, formally known as {{nihongo|Fudarakusan Jigen-ji Kannon-in|補陀落山慈眼寺観音院||}}, is a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in Uemachi district of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]], {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Kannon-in was built early in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603 – 1868) and is noted for its Edo-style {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|qid=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}}.[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} ==History== ===Founding=== Kannon-in built in the early Edo period and its history is closely related to that of the {{ill|Ikeda clan|en|Ikeda clan|qid=Q2975277|lt=Ikeda clan|1=Ikeda clan}}. {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Tadakatsu]]|池田忠雄|}} (1602 – 1632), ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|qid=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain}} in {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|qid=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} and lord of {{ill|Okayama Castle|en|Okayama Castle|qid=Q1013448|lt=Okayama Castle|1=Okayama Castle}}, died at a young age and was succeeded by his 3 year old son {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Mitsunaka]]|池田光仲}} (1630 – 1693). The [[Tokugawa shogunate]] named the infant Mitsunaka ''daimyō'' of {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}} in {{ill|Hōki Province|en|Hōki Province|qid=Q1053703|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|qid=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} provinces.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Mitsunaka |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} In 1632, soon after Mitsunaka's accession to the position of daimyō, {{Nihongo|Senden|専伝}}, the fourth chief priest of [[Kōchin-ji]] in the present-day Okayama, sent his disciple Gōben to build a temple for the Ikeda clan. Senden ordered Gōben to build the temple in the scenic Kuritani area of present-day Tottori City,[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/7-kannonin.html 補陀絡山慈眼寺観音院]{{in lang|ja}} and charged him with building prayer temple for the veneration of the {{Nihongo|Shōkanzeon Bosatsu|聖観世音菩薩}}, or [[Kannon|Kannon]] {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}. The temple was named Kannon-in. A statue of the Kannon Bodhisattva, reputably carved from rock from the mountain of {{ill|Tottori Castle|en|Tottori Castle|qid=Q1073699|lt=Tottori Castle|1=Tottori Castle}} by the {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} (668 – 749), a Buddhist priest of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, was bestowed on the temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 ) |title=Kannnon-in |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-29 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} ===Move to Uemachi site=== In 1639 the temple was moved to its present location in the Uemachi of present-day Tottori City to serve as a temple for the use of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}. Mitsunaka was deeply devoted to the Kannon Bodhisattva, and became a patron of the temple. At this time the extensive {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|伽藍}} temple complex Kannon-in was planned and built, including its well-known garden. At this time Kannon-in was formally renamed Fudarakusan Jigen-ji Kannon-in. Mitsunaka's oldest son, the second lord of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}, named Kannon-in a {{Nihongo|''kiganji''|祈願寺}} prayer temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Tsunakiyo |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The temple attained the high status of one of the {{Nihongo|eight prayer temples|八ヶ寺|hachigatera}} of the domain, a status it would retain throughout the Edo period. ===Later history=== After the abolition of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|qid=Q841985|lt=han|1=Han system}} system Kannon-in ceased to be a temple under the patronage of the Ikeda clan, and the temple lost its rice stipend and any form of monetary support. Soon after local adherents of Kannon-in took over the financial support of the temple, support that continues to the present. The Kannon-in garden was designated as a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|qid=Q20982902|lt=Place of Scenic Beauty|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} by the Japanese government in 1937.[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] == Garden of Kannon-in == The garden of Kannon-in was built is an example of an Edo period Japanese garden in the {{Nihongo|''Chisenkanshō-shiki teien''|池泉観賞式庭園|}} style, which literally means a garden of the "pond appreciation style". The route stretches across the [[:en:Chūgoku Region|Chūgoku Region]] of western Japan from {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane|1=Shimane Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}} prefectures.[http://www.kannon.org/reijyo/01/index.htm 名刹古刹37ヶ寺中国観音霊場とは]{{in lang|ja}} {{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mani-ji]]
(special)}} {{s-ttl|title=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage |years='''Kannon-in'''
#32}} {{s-aft|after=[[Daiun-in]]

(city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.49608889|N|134.24155|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|1=Avalokiteśvara}}|part_of={{ill|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q987974|1=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Tottori, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kannon-in | native_name = 観音院 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kannonin Tottori22s4110.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Garden of Kannon-in | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = 1 | map_caption = Kannon-in | location = 162 Uemachi, {{ill|Tottori (city)|en|Tottori (city)|qid=Q200731|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori (city)}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|29|45.92|N|134|14|29.58|E|display=inline|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai Buddhism|1=Tendai}} | deity = {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q193849|lt=Shōkannon|1=Avalokiteśvara}} | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm}} | founded_by = | year_completed = 1632 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Place of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Kannonin Tottori06s4s4440.jpg|thumb|Sho-in study hall]] [[File:Japan Tottori KannonIn DSC01905.jpg|thumb|Garden of Kannon-in]] [[File:Kannonin Tottori01o4592.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hondō|Hondō]] main hall of Kannon-in]] [[File:Kannonin Tottori04n4592.jpg|thumb|[[Sanmon]] gates of Kannon-in]] {{nihongo|'''Kannon-in'''|観音院|Kannon-in}}, formally known as {{nihongo|Fudarakusan Jigen-ji Kannon-in|補陀落山慈眼寺観音院||}}, is a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in Uemachi district of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]], {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Kannon-in was built early in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603 – 1868) and is noted for its Edo-style {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|qid=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}}.[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} ==History== ===Founding=== Kannon-in built in the early Edo period and its history is closely related to that of the {{ill|Ikeda clan|en|Ikeda clan|qid=Q2975277|lt=Ikeda clan|1=Ikeda clan}}. {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Tadakatsu]]|池田忠雄|}} (1602 – 1632), ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|qid=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain}} in {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|qid=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} and lord of {{ill|Okayama Castle|en|Okayama Castle|qid=Q1013448|lt=Okayama Castle|1=Okayama Castle}}, died at a young age and was succeeded by his 3 year old son {{Nihongo|[[Ikeda Mitsunaka]]|池田光仲}} (1630 – 1693). The [[Tokugawa shogunate]] named the infant Mitsunaka ''daimyō'' of {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}} in {{ill|Hōki Province|en|Hōki Province|qid=Q1053703|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|qid=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} provinces.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Mitsunaka |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} In 1632, soon after Mitsunaka's accession to the position of daimyō, {{Nihongo|Senden|専伝}}, the fourth chief priest of [[Kōchin-ji]] in the present-day Okayama, sent his disciple Gōben to build a temple for the Ikeda clan. Senden ordered Gōben to build the temple in the scenic Kuritani area of present-day Tottori City,[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/7-kannonin.html 補陀絡山慈眼寺観音院]{{in lang|ja}} and charged him with building prayer temple for the veneration of the {{Nihongo|Shōkanzeon Bosatsu|聖観世音菩薩}}, or [[Kannon|Kannon]] {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}. The temple was named Kannon-in. A statue of the Kannon Bodhisattva, reputably carved from rock from the mountain of {{ill|Tottori Castle|en|Tottori Castle|qid=Q1073699|lt=Tottori Castle|1=Tottori Castle}} by the {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} (668 – 749), a Buddhist priest of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, was bestowed on the temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 ) |title=Kannnon-in |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-29 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} ===Move to Uemachi site=== In 1639 the temple was moved to its present location in the Uemachi of present-day Tottori City to serve as a temple for the use of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}. Mitsunaka was deeply devoted to the Kannon Bodhisattva, and became a patron of the temple. At this time the extensive {{nihongo|''shichidō garan''|伽藍}} temple complex Kannon-in was planned and built, including its well-known garden. At this time Kannon-in was formally renamed Fudarakusan Jigen-ji Kannon-in. Mitsunaka's oldest son, the second lord of the {{ill|Tottori Domain|en|Tottori Domain|qid=Q905521|lt=Tottori Domain|1=Tottori Domain}}, named Kannon-in a {{Nihongo|''kiganji''|祈願寺}} prayer temple.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Jinmei Daijiten (日本人名大辞典) |title=Ikeda Tsunakiyo |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-03-17 |year=2011 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The temple attained the high status of one of the {{Nihongo|eight prayer temples|八ヶ寺|hachigatera}} of the domain, a status it would retain throughout the Edo period. ===Later history=== After the abolition of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|qid=Q841985|lt=han|1=Han system}} system Kannon-in ceased to be a temple under the patronage of the Ikeda clan, and the temple lost its rice stipend and any form of monetary support. Soon after local adherents of Kannon-in took over the financial support of the temple, support that continues to the present. The Kannon-in garden was designated as a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|qid=Q20982902|lt=Place of Scenic Beauty|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} by the Japanese government in 1937.[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] == Garden of Kannon-in == The garden of Kannon-in was built is an example of an Edo period Japanese garden in the {{Nihongo|''Chisenkanshō-shiki teien''|池泉観賞式庭園|}} style, which literally means a garden of the "pond appreciation style". A ''Chisenkanshō-shiki'' garden is meant to be viewed from a fixed perspective from a single location, rather than a garden to stroll through and view from several angles.[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/c/chisenkanshoushikiteien.htm Chisen Kanshoushiki Teien] In the case of the Kannon-in the garden is viewed from the {{ill|Veranda|en|Veranda|qid=Q693922|lt=Veranda|1=Veranda}} of the {{Nihongo|''shoin''|書院|}}, a hall used for the study of Buddhist {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}}s. Work on the garden began in 1650, and took ten years to complete. The garden utilizes the gentle slope of the landscape of Kannon-in. A depiction of the garden is found the {{Nihongo|''Kannon-in ezu''|観音院絵図}}, or illustration of Kannon-in, published in the {{Nihongo|無駄安留記|Mudaaruki}} in 1858. The ''Mudaaruki'' is probably based on earlier works.[http://www.rs.tottori-u.ac.jp/ibaraki/mudaaruki/ ]{{in lang|ja}} The {{Nihongo|bekkan|別館}} annex structure in the garden is in a slightly different location in the ''Mudaaruki'' than what is seen in the garden today, as the Kannon-in garden was probably restructured during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. ==Branch temples== Kannon-in has two branch temples. *{{Nihongo|Seikyō-ji|清鏡寺}}, Tachikawachō, Tottori City *{{Nihongo|Enjō-ji|円城}}, Kokufuchō, Tottori City == Transportation == *8 minutes by taxi from {{ill|Tottori Station|en|Tottori Station|qid=Q858295|lt=Tottori Station|1=Tottori Station}}, {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=JR West|1=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Sanin Main Line|Sanin Main Line]] *4 minute walk from the {{Nihongo|Yamanote Kaikan-mae Stop|山の手会館前}}, {{Nihongo|[[Kururi Bus Line]]|くる梨}} {{Nihongo|Red Course|赤コース}} from Tottori Station == See also == * For an explanation of terms used see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == Order in Buddhist pilgrimage == Kannon-in is the 32nd of the {{ill|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q987974|lt=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage}}, a ''[[junrei]]'' pilgrimage route established in 1981 of 33 Buddhist temples in the dedicated to the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} Kannon. The route stretches across the [[:en:Chūgoku Region|Chūgoku Region]] of western Japan from {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane|1=Shimane Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}} prefectures.[http://www.kannon.org/reijyo/01/index.htm 名刹古刹37ヶ寺中国観音霊場とは]{{in lang|ja}} {{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mani-ji]]
(special)}} {{s-ttl|title=Chūgoku 33 Kannon Pilgrimage |years='''Kannon-in'''
#32}} {{s-aft|after=[[Daiun-in]]
#33}} {{s-end}} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|en|List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|qid=Q6594211|lt=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)|1=List of Places of Scenic Beauty of Japan (Tottori)}} ==References== {{reflist|33em}} ==External links== *[http://www.kannon.org/jiin/01/36/index.htm 補陀落山 慈眼寺 観音院]{{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/top/multilang/English/sightseeing/kanko_en.html Tottori City Sightseeing: Kannon-in Garden] {{Commons category|Jigen-ji Kannon-in}} {{wikidata link|Q3192708|arz|كانون ان|co|commons:Category:Jigen-ji Kannon-in|en|Kannon-in|fr|Kannon-in|ja|観音院|ko|간논인}} [[Category:1632 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:17th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Gardens in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Tendai temples]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
